segments.text: It's 14, Valentine's Day, sitting in the basement of the Weissenbaker building,
getting ready to interview Charlie Holland, who got here a little more than 50 years ago.
Charlie, test your mic.
Testing one, two.
Is it all right?
Charlie, for someone who may not have known you in a few years to come
when they're looking at this material or listening to it,
would you begin by summarizing your background and experience,
where you grew up, went to school, your experience before you got to Texas A&M?
Well, I grew up in western North Carolina, and my dad was a Baptist minister.
In fact, I started the first grade the year he finished Wake Forest.
He had been in World War I, and he decided to go down and finish up.
So we moved to Wake Forest, and about Christmas time,
the 21st of May, I was in the first grade.
I was in the first grade.
My twins were born, and he took me out of the first grade
because he was afraid I might bring home some childhood diseases.
And then the next year after that, we moved to Mountain View,
and I was just recently at Christmas going up there looking for the place that we moved to.
It was on a dirt road out from North Wilkesboro.
And this will kind of give you the view of what things were like in those times.
And he was pastor of a little Baptist church there,
and it was a Baptist junior college high school.
And he taught the mathematics there.
And this was back in time that the Depression was coming on us out there.
And all he got out of that year's teaching at that little junior college
was an Underwood typewriter, which I have at home.
That was all they could pay.
And I went to school.
It was a little one-room school.
It was a little schoolhouse.
First grade in the first row, second grade in the second.
Pot-bellied stove.
Down at the bottom on old slick red hill was an outhouse.
And all the kids went to the outhouse.
And also the water was simply a barrel just like you'd see a keg of beer.
And so Dad said, when we went there, I better put you back in the first grade
because I don't want you to get behind.
So I started out in the first.
Well, in that school, you could get.
Promoted.
All you had to do was just show you could do the second grade work.
So before the year was over, I was over in the second grade.
Second row.
They'd moved me to the second.
So you just changed seats.
You didn't know.
No paperwork.
Before the end of the year, I was in the third row.
Well, then we moved to Stony Point, which is about 60 miles from Charlotte.
And it's still very primitive in those days.
But it did have a consolidated school.
They bused all the kids from the county into that school.
So Dad said, well, I know you were in the third grade there,
but I think I'll put you back and let you do the second over.
I don't want you to get behind.
Well, they put me in that second grade, and I stayed the whole year
because they didn't know how to get me out of it even though I knew it.
Well, right after Christmas, we went back there and was looking for this.
We wanted to go to all the places that we'd lived.
And so we went to North Wilkesboro, and there was a bypass,
and we stopped at a McDonald's.
No.
This Mountain View wasn't even on the map yet.
And so we went there to stop at a McDonald's,
and I saw a well-dressed lady over there.
And I went over and struck up a conversation with her.
And I asked her if she knew where Mountain View was.
She said, oh, it's just right up here.
You turn off right up the road here.
Now, let me point out that in those years,
Wilkes County was noted to be the biggest white lightning county in the United States.
And so.
She said, yes, I went to school there, one little one-room schoolhouse outhouse down at the bottom of the hill.
And so then I went on back and sat down, and she came back over afterwards.
And she told my daughter, or my sister, that her husband had passed away.
And she says, well, what did he do?
She says, oh, he was a bootlegger.
Says that was the only way to make a living in those years.
She said, well, did he do anything?
She said, well, did he do anything?
Didn't the law get after him?
She said, oh, yes, he built time five different times.
So this was kind of the area that I grew up in.
And my dad and I, he was always gone a lot and so forth.
And we just didn't have these father-to-son talks that you always have nowadays.
You'll hear of, like Andy Hardy and Judge Stone.
The closest we ever came to it was,
the day we were packing the car,
he was getting ready to take me off to this little Baptist Junior College,
Mars Hill, out from Nashville, North Carolina.
And it was always assumed that I'd go to college.
It was never even, we just didn't discuss it.
And I delivered papers there, and I knew all the professional people in town,
and they said, you've got, there's a lot of emphasis there on you need to,
you need to go to college and make something yourself.
You heard that over and over again.
And so, as we were packing the car, dad said to me, he says,
son, what are you going to study when you get up there?
I said, well, dad, I thought I'd study dentistry.
He said, no, son, I don't think I'd do that if I were you.
I don't believe you'll ever be able to keep up with your tools.
Well, see, I'd lost his saw and hammer so many times that he was just,
and he said, why don't you just go ahead and study engineering?
Well, that's what I did.
And so, after the pre-engineering course up there,
Mars Hill, I went to North Carolina.
And my junior year, I was having breakfast,
and that's when Pearl Harbor hit.
Actually, the day we were packing the car,
the Germans marched in Europe, which marked the beginning,
which is the day I started to college.
And then when I was in my junior year at North Carolina State,
the Germans, the Pearl Harbor occurred.
So I went down, and I didn't want to get drafted.
I wanted to finish school.
So what I did was they had a program there where you could become,
get a direct commission.
Even though you hadn't been in ROTC, they had an ROTC there,
and people marched around all the time.
And they told me I wasn't in point in taking it
because I could only do two years of it, so I didn't take it.
But I went down and got a direct commission to go in the Navy.
And so as soon as I finished college, I had to go in the Navy.
And I was in the Navy and on destroyers for about,
just nearly four years was the tour date.
And the roughest duty that we had was on Okinawa.
We went up there.
We went up there with nine destroyers in the squadron.
And this is where the kamikazes.
And for about two or three months, we were exposed to kamikazes every day.
And we lost six out of our nine destroyers.
There were only three of us that made it back.
And it was just a matter of luck.
We weren't that much better.
And we just, when they'd hit one station, we'd be on another one.
Or the plane that was coming in at you, we got it shot down.
Well, then after the war,
I thought about going to Georgia Tech,
but it turned out that there was no housing down there.
So I decided I'd work for Burlington Mills.
And they had put me into a little program
that was supposed to be training the plant managers of the future.
Well, it turned out that it looked like I was going to have to work
on the third shift for about 10 years, 15.
So I decided I'd just go back to graduate school
and went back to North Carolina State.
And Dr. Lindsey had taught down there.
And so they gave me his name.
I called him.
And got in touch with him.
And so we came out here.
And I began studying the graduate program in chemical engineering
in January of 1948.
That's it.
You came to Texas A&M?
Texas A&M in January 1948.
And I promised my wife, because we'd been out here
when I was on the destroyer,
I promised her we'd never stay in Texas.
As soon as I got my degree, we were leaving.
Well, we stayed.
Still here.
Describe what was...
what Texas A&M was physically.
What was...
Okay.
What buildings...
I'll give you the whole picture.
I have some...
A guy named Proctor was one of the first heads
of the extension service, as I understand.
And he was my brother-in-law's...
my brother-in-law's mother's father.
He used to be over here in this same place of research.
He used to have a picture over there.
So I... We visited with the...
Mr. Lee's on the way.
And Mr. Lee had been grad...
had been taught...
He'd been a high school principal.
He'd gone to the old school and studied the Latin.
He was a classical type individual.
And he said...
So he called up Bolton.
Bolton was the executive vice president.
Said, get this...
get him a room, a place to stay up there.
So after I'd already come here and gotten settled,
I was over in Lindsey's office one day.
And Mr. Bolton called up and he said he wanted to see me.
Boy, they were just shaking over there.
I went over.
And...
So he told me.
But the amusing thing is...
And then he ended up saying, well, just go over here and...
Oh, I've forgotten...
Boyette, go see him.
He's in charge of housing.
And that was what I got out of him.
About a month or so, we were down there visiting with Mr. Lee again.
He said...
He said, well, I suppose you know Bolton well by now.
I said, I'm sorry, Mr. Lee.
Our paths haven't crossed any since that one time I met him.
But Dr. Lindsey was head of the Chemical Engineering Department.
We were over there in the old Petroleum Building.
At that time, Barlow was Dean of Engineering.
And Saul Wright was head of Civil.
Charlie Crawford was head of Mechanical.
Let's see.
Shepard was Dean of Agriculture.
Petroleum?
No, Petroleum.
Harold Vance was head of Petroleum.
And Shepard was head of Agriculture.
And I don't remember...
I remember Mr. Porter was head of the Math Department.
But I don't know...
I don't remember now who was the Science Dean.
I think Pearson was Dean of the Graduate College,
followed later by Trotter.
But Pearson, I believe, was the Dean when I came here.
I worked most of the time, dealt with Trotter's group over there.
And I think that's...
Let's see.
Street was teaching drawing.
And I can't remember the fellow.
It was head of Aero.
And that's about the one.
There were no sidewalks.
It was all...
Actually, it was the same way it was in 1943.
They had done nothing during the war.
And what I came in, we had already gotten over the flood here,
but there's still a lot of veterans.
And I would say that that was the toughest group to compete with I've ever ran against
because all they want to know is what's required.
And we'll do it.
Because you and your other veteran buddies,
you were mentally, emotionally mature.
Ready to go to work.
Ready to go to work.
You'd had experience.
And also, you could separate...
I had a family, too.
Everything was riding on it.
What was your graduate student stipend or assistantship or what?
Oh, it was probably about $100 a month.
Plus the GI Bill.
I'm not even sure we got $100.
Maybe we did.
I don't remember what it was.
But it was living in College View.
We could make it.
But, see, by the time I got ready to finish,
I ran out of the...
I took a job teaching.
I ran out of the GI Bill.
So I had to take a job teaching.
And then I had to buy a house over in College Hills.
And I bought one on Harrington Street over there.
It cost $13,700.
And that was the poorest year that I've ever experienced in my life.
My salary was $370.
$370 a month.
And Lindsey had to get special dispensation to get me $370.
And so that's kind of what it was like here then.
Now, when did you switch officially in paperwork
from being a graduate student to being a faculty member?
About 52.
Okay.
So you're... All right.
We're still talking about the graduate student days.
Yeah.
Well, I finished...
I got my PhD in 53, but I'd already...
Oh, okay.
I took a job as instructor.
In 52, I think it was.
And then I believe in...
I guess in 53, that fall, I went ahead...
I've forgotten whether I got promoted to assistant professor.
Maybe I went a year before I got made assistant professor.
Looking back now with the clear vision of hindsight,
what courses and what teachers,
instructors, were the most important to you?
Well, actually, we had some things back then and there
that are better than they are now.
And that is that we took courses outside the department,
a lot of them.
And I took mathematics.
I had Luther.
I had Bayes.
And then Dr. Klippel and I, years later, we worked on...
I'd just go over with him and spend an hour a day
every once in a while.
Every week, we'd have a meeting
and prove theorems and so on.
And chemistry, Jensen and Dr. Watson.
I worked under Murdoch, Phil Murdoch in chemical engineering,
but Watson was one of them.
Another one that I took course over there was POTS,
organic chemistry.
And Kenny Hancock was another one that I took a course under.
I also took a physics course and Dr.
Weeks taught it.
And then after I finished my coursework,
I went back and took another course in kinetic theory under Dr. Silvey.
Which goes way back.
And old Dr. Silvey, the first day, there were three of us in there
registered for his course.
And he, two physics students and me.
And he said, he said,
Mr. Tarlin, would you like to participate in the classroom discussion?
And I said, well, I believe I'd like to do that.
Well, what that amounted to, we were presenting one-third of that course
for audit.
Mm-hmm.
And then I also had there one under Dr.
Weeks over there.
He was the first one.
I took x-rays and crystallography, and I never had vectors.
And he just, he had the course full of it, but I learned it, picked it up from him.
And I had to compete against two graduate physics students in there, or three.
And so Jensen was an outstanding chemistry teacher.
Watson was an outstanding chemistry teacher.
In fact, all of those people,
Potts was never a real outstanding teacher.
He had a tremendous amount of knowledge,
but he could never finish one sentence until he started another one.
Now, what was his first name?
Because I remember Dr. Potts in agriculture.
No, this was a different Potts.
I don't remember what his first name is.
You just have to look it up.
What department?
Chemistry.
In the chemistry department?
He was the organic prof in chemistry.
Kenny Hancock taught a good course in chemistry.
All right, what about within chemical engineering?
Who were the key?
Phil Murdock, I took a course under him, and let's see, you know that, I may have taken
most all the courses I took under him.
I took one under GL Fair.
He taught process control, and I took that.
But we took, in those years, we were really taking, see we didn't teach our own mathematics
in our department, and now all these people trying to do, teach everything in one department,
they never see the light of day outside the walls of their department.
Did anybody, do you remember any advice that somebody gave you, which later proved to
be very valuable advice to a chemical engineer?
Oh, I don't know.
I just kind of stumbled along, I guess.
I very nearly quit shortly after, about 1955, because I went down to Exxon, and that worked
one summer, and they wanted me to stay with Exxon.
And at that time, I was in the lab.
I was in the lab.
I was making $5,000 a year, Dr. Harris was making $5,100 a year, and Dr. Lindsey was making
$5,200 a year.
And I was an assistant professor, and both of them were full, and I figured, well, why
should I stay here when that's all I've got, that's all the future I've got?
So it was, Shirley Lynch, incidentally, in that old building, was head of geology over
there, and that.
What persuaded you to stay?
Well, I just got to thinking about it that, I don't know.
What had happened was, I had to hurry up and get through, because that one year, Phil Murdock
left and Watson left, and all my committee left, so I really pushed that to get it completed.
I wasn't really sure whether I wanted to stay, I guess, in that corporate structure.
You don't have nears much freedom.
What was your dissertation topic and your area of specialization?
This was, I'd done my Masters on distillation, and this one was on catalytic, study of catalyst.
And so on my committee I had Dr. Potts from biology, or Potter.
Potter from biology.
And Dr.
Weeks, and Dr. Watson, and Phil Murdock.
Yes, that's right.
I believe were the ones on it and I couldn't get that arranged for the final meeting and I went by Watson's house one
Afternoon late and he was sitting back in the backyard of the shirt off drinking beer
And I told him I don't know that I'm gonna be able to graduate this time because I can't get this committee together
Jordan says oh I can take care of that for you
And he went in and called him up and we met over in the old patrolling building that night
He set it up and we all met over there and I got it over with so I made it
Do you remember any special questions on that?
That final oral exam.
I don't remember.
I just remember that
They asked me some question and I guess it's when you're working on some of those things you got so many
Details in your mind.
It's some little simple something
You won't see
And they asked me that and then they found the weakness and so they just I don't remember what it was
but then they just
One after another, you know, and that a part I remember if you know it
Let's go on to the next, you know, but if you ever find something that you didn't know
As you Thought about it then and now look back
How did our chemical engineering department?
How did our strength compare?
nationally in the mid 50s
Oh We were really a young department then we and in fact, we got down
Because the salaries were so poor
that at one time
We were down to just Dr. Harry's
Dr. Lindsay and I and we had to get some graduate students help us out
So it's all we could do to keep keep the doors open.
You're saying three faculty we got down to three faculty
Everybody quit because salaries they went to industry or somewhere
Because salaries were so poor back in those days and that's about when I was about ready to throw in the towel
Was then when where the real change come was when John Connolly got in there salaries went up hours went up 30% a year
For three or four years there, but now let's let's back up
John Calhoun came here as Dean in the 50s did Marlowe was head it was Dean when I came here
Okay, and then John Calhoun came next
All right Do you remember any early impressions of Calhoun as the Dean?
I just remember that he was much more energetic than
Well Barlow was energetic enough except that I thought about Calhoun was a much better individual and accountant and Barlow was
What did Calhoun did he come over and visit did he become acquainted with y'all?
Oh, yeah, he had us over
He had me over to his office
And I think he was kind of leery of old dr. Lindsay
and Doc had already told him Calhoun told me he says right here's my
Territory no, there's your don't you get in here?
Don't you come across it, but he did ask me one time
He had me in his office talking.
I think I irritated him because he said to me he said
Wait, you're saying he is this Calhoun.
That's Calhoun.
Okay.
He won't know of any
Any a the
dis Incentives or negative things about Lindsay.
Mm-hmm.
I said well even if I had some I wouldn't tell you
So that ended that discussion I may have heard his feeling but because I was pretty loyal to the old doc
Was did the best he could with what he had and he didn't have much to work with
Now the enrollment I'm assuming in engineering in the 50s was going up.
Well, no actually what happened was enrollment of everybody's went down to about
1955 was a low point.
Oh, okay.
I was after the war is over and this is about in there somewhere
Maybe even after that they decided that they wanted to make it optional core a little bit
And I think we went on that program for a little while and the Roman started going up
Then we went back to compulsory for in it.
It started going down again
So the enrollment never you just do everything you wanted to and you couldn't end up with much more than two sections of about 20 some
each and Y'all still had the graduate program.
Oh, yeah during those dry years and
I presume that industry was generally hiring the bright
Youngsters when they graduated we had we had they were
We were always able to place them and we helped place all those people
the only year that I couldn't get them placed was I believe in about
1980 when enrollment had just gone out of proportion and at the same times
The job market dropped completely so we had a real problem then
all right, but then Benson was after bar
Calhoun really wasn't Dean very long right now.
He moves up to what?
Oh
Was he a system?
He was went up to a vice presidential letter vice president for engineering, okay, right
When did you first meet Fred Benson?
Well, I met him
before he became Dean I knew him because we were
both in a naval reserve unit together, and he wasn't very,
and we actually were taking one of the math courses there together at one time.
He was taking one course, a lower one, than I was taking.
Did he do his degree here?
Yeah, he got his B.S.
at Kansas,
and then he got a master's several years back here, as I recall,
and then he was taking some additional courses.
Describe Fred Benson as a human being, as a personality.
Well, we got along just fine.
One of the things, when I got appointed, what he did was call me in one afternoon,
and he said, Charlie, I'd like for you to take over here as head of the department.
I said, now, you don't have to tell me right now.
You can wait until a little later and tell me if you think it over.
And so I did, and I wasn't really sure I wanted to.
I really wanted to tell him, how about getting old Doc to take it one more year?
I'd like to do the things I'm doing a little longer.
But I decided to go ahead and take it because I thought that I could probably do more things
that I wanted to do if I were head than I could if they got somebody from the outside
and he started giving me all kinds of directions.
When was that?
What year?
That was in 1964.
All right.
At that point, you said things you wanted to do.
What were your goals?
I'd gone to Exxon in 1955 and gotten started modeling.
I was doing distillation on computers and actually setting up programs to solve problems on them.
And I worked with them, consulted with them, and I had graduate students.
We'd try out new methods, and we had an old 650, and that's what they had down at Exxon.
And we had an old 650, and they put it up in the old EE building.
Now, this is an old key punch?
Yeah, yeah.
Punch card?
In the building.
And my graduate students would go up there and use it at night.
And it had a fire escape, and so they'd go up the fire escape and run.
And we'd run that thing all night long.
That's what we did.
That's the way we got our time.
And we had a lot of that going on.
I'd written a book and a good many papers on it of methods that we had devised to solve these problems.
So this was, at this point, primarily petrochemically related?
Yeah.
Or you can do chemical either one.
It's used in both fields, not just one.
So, I think the way that you're thinking about this is,
and we're going to get back to Benson.
Describe him.
What did he like and not like?
How did he operate?
Well, he, I think, although he would claim that he wanted everybody to say what they wanted to,
he really wanted to hear the right words.
And he had around him people that he knew would always back him up.
I remember I used to go in there to budget hearings.
And he'd have Ed Finner.
He'd have old Bain, Spencer, and he'd have two or three others like that.
And he'd say something and they'd all chime in and agree.
But he and I got along pretty well because I never did go in and ask him to solve my
problems for me.
I went ahead and solved the things.
The only time he and I got crosswise was he was pushing real hard for this Doctor of Engineering
program.
And he had all of us department heads in there one day.
And what he was wanting us to do was agree not to weaken down the B.S.
degree to where
it would not be an accredited degree and have the Doctor of Engineering be the first accredited
degree.
And I stood up against him because I told him that we could not afford to have a large
group of students coming out of here without an accredited degree.
If we're going to give a B.S.
degree, we'd better make it accredited.
And he got so mad at me that he walked out and stayed gone for about fifteen minutes.
And then we finally decided we'd have it kind of optional.
If they wanted, we'd always have the B.S.
accredited, but if they wanted to go at an
early stage somewhere over to the Doctor of Engineering, they could.
And so we'd just leave it where whichever one won out would be the way we'd go.
And I told them we were just not big enough.
To fight against the whole United States on one program like that, they'd run us out
of business.
So he and I, and then later he says, Charlie, you can do whatever you want to.
He didn't.
But he told me many times that I had the best department in the College of Engineering.
How long in years was that Doctor of Engineering degree planned to be?
Oh, it was slightly longer, I think, than a master's.
And it was made up of coursework plus a proper degree.
Oh, okay.
I didn't think it was a real problem in the industry.
It was actually a real good degree.
It might fit engineering better than some of the real, because when you get over too
far in the research, you're nearly like a chemist or a physicist.
And you could make a good program out of that where you would actually do research on things
that pertain to engineering per se more than you can on some of the directions they're
going in, because they get toward the science end of it, and then you're dealing with the
of the chemists and so on so it was a good degree it was just that i didn't think that we could
we could swing it all right what did happen to it well there's still it's uh i think they still give
some of them that but it there just wasn't the demand for it it's just hard to sell
um all right so so there was a key decision uh that i had something to do with all right and
that saved him there really in benson's personality did he laugh much did he no he was
always just in the meetings there was never much said and uh he got to the point pretty quickly
didn't he oh yeah yeah and it was uh it was well thought out anything he did was well thought out
he was good to work for and i didn't have a bit of trouble working for him we just had
i mean he knew that i wasn't back down anymore
i i expressed my opinion he didn't like it but he respected it and then he told me later i think he
really it hurt him that he couldn't handle it better than he did in your early years as department
head who were your key associates uh as department heads elsewhere in the college in engineering and
anywhere else oh well actually in those years i knew all the department heads real well but who
were the ones you worked with
uh most well i worked i knew some man real well he and i were well
acquainted describe him well he was uh very precise uh and very fair and uh
i had a lot of respect for him and uh he did for me and what what describe him physically
